title: Polypharmacy, Potentially Inappropriate Medications (Beers Criteria), and a Prescribing Cascade in an 80-Year-Old Woman Admitted After a Fall with Orthostatic Hypotension, Recurrent Hypoglycemia, and New Confusion: Comprehensive Interprofessional Medication Reconciliation, Anticholinergic-Burden Reduction, Supervised Deprescribing, and Safe Transition-of-Care Teaching
subtitle: POLYPHARMACY = the concurrent use of multiple medications, commonly defined as ≥5 regularly scheduled drugs (HYPERPOLYPHARMACY ≥10); extremely common in older adults with multimorbidity and a leading, often PREVENTABLE driver of adverse drug events (ADEs), falls, delirium/cognitive impairment, nonadherence, drug-drug and drug-disease interactions, prescribing cascades, hospitalizations, and increased mortality. WHY OLDER ADULTS ARE VULNERABLE — altered PHARMACOKINETICS (reduced renal clearance [lower GFR even with 'normal-looking' creatinine because of low muscle mass — estimate with CrCl/eGFR + dose renally], reduced hepatic metabolism, reduced serum ALBUMIN → more free active drug for highly protein-bound drugs, increased fat:water ratio → longer half-life for lipophilic drugs like diazepam, reduced total body water → higher levels of hydrophilic drugs) and altered PHARMACODYNAMICS (increased sensitivity to CNS depressants, anticoagulants, anticholinergics, and to hypotensive/hypoglycemic effects); 'start low, go slow' dosing. PRESCRIBING CASCADE = a new drug prescribed to treat what is actually an ADVERSE EFFECT of an existing drug, mistaken for a new medical condition — each new drug adds its own risks; classic examples: dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (amlodipine) → peripheral EDEMA → misread as fluid overload → loop DIURETIC (→ orthostasis, hypokalemia, incontinence); metoclopramide/antipsychotic → drug-induced PARKINSONISM → levodopa; NSAID → raised BP → new antihypertensive; cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil) → urinary urgency → ANTICHOLINERGIC (oxybutynin) which then worsens cognition; diuretic → gout/hyperuricemia → urate-lowering therapy. Recognizing and UNWINDING cascades (treat the cause = stop the offending drug) is central. BEERS CRITERIA (American Geriatrics Society) = an evidence-based list of POTENTIALLY INAPPROPRIATE MEDICATIONS (PIMs) to avoid or use with caution in adults ≥65 because risks usually outweigh benefits; high-yield PIMs: BENZODIAZEPINES + 'Z-drugs' (zolpidem) — falls, fractures, sedation, cognitive impairment, delirium; FIRST-GENERATION ANTIHISTAMINES (diphenhydramine) and other strong ANTICHOLINERGICS (oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants like amitriptyline, scopolamine, cyclobenzaprine) — confusion, delirium, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, falls; LONG-ACTING SULFONYLUREAS (glyburide) — prolonged HYPOGLYCEMIA; SLIDING-SCALE INSULIN as sole regimen; NSAIDs — GI bleeding, AKI, fluid retention/HF; muscle relaxants; ANTIPSYCHOTICS for behavioral symptoms of dementia (increased stroke/mortality — boxed warning); long-term PPIs; DIGOXIN >0.125 mg/day; peripheral alpha-1 blockers (doxazosin) for hypertension (orthostasis). ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN = the cumulative anticholinergic effect of multiple drugs (each may be 'mild') → additive risk of delirium, cognitive decline, falls, constipation, urinary retention, dry mouth/eyes — quantified by tools (e.g., Anticholinergic Cognitive Burden scale); reducing total burden is a key intervention. STOPP/START criteria = complementary explicit criteria: STOPP (Screening Tool of Older Persons' Prescriptions — drugs to consider stopping) and START (Screening Tool to Alert to Right Treatment — PRESCRIBING OMISSIONS to consider starting where indicated, e.g., a statin or ACE inhibitor with clear indication, calcium/vitamin D + bone-protective therapy in osteoporosis, indicated anticoagulation) — because under-prescribing of beneficial drugs is also a problem. MEDICATION RECONCILIATION = the formal process of building the most accurate possible list of EVERYTHING the patient actually takes (prescription, OTC, herbals/supplements, PRNs, eye drops, inhalers, topicals — with name, dose, route, frequency, and indication) and COMPARING it against new orders at EVERY transition of care (admission, transfer, discharge) to detect and resolve omissions, duplications, dosing errors, and interactions; it is a Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal; use MULTIPLE SOURCES (the patient, family/caregiver, the actual bottles / 'brown-bag' review, the community pharmacy, the PCP/prior records, the state prescription-monitoring database) because any single source is often incomplete or wrong. DEPRESCRIBING = the planned, supervised, patient-centered process of reducing or stopping medications that may be causing harm or no longer provide benefit relative to the patient's goals and life expectancy — prioritize by risk/benefit, change ONE thing at a time when possible, and TAPER drugs with withdrawal/rebound potential (benzodiazepines, opioids, gabapentinoids, beta-blockers, clonidine, SSRIs/SNRIs, PPIs, corticosteroids) rather than stopping abruptly; monitor for withdrawal and for return of the original condition; align with patient PREFERENCES and goals (some prioritize quality of life/symptom control over long-term preventive medications). TRANSITIONS OF CARE are the highest-risk period for medication errors and readmissions → at discharge: produce a single reconciled, plain-language medication list (including what was STOPPED/CHANGED and why), use TEACH-BACK, address adherence barriers (cost, regimen complexity, dexterity/arthritis with caps/inhalers, cognition, vision, health literacy, language) with strategies (simplify/consolidate dosing, pill organizers, blister packs, combination products, reminders, caregiver involvement), communicate the changes to the PCP and community pharmacy, and arrange timely follow-up. INTERPROFESSIONAL + PATIENT-CENTERED: PHARMACIST-led medication review, geriatrician/PCP, nurse, social work, and the patient/caregiver with shared decision-making — the nurse is pivotal in obtaining an accurate history, recognizing ADEs/cascades, reconciling at transitions, teaching, and advocating for simplification

patient.name: R. Whitfield
patient.code: Full code (per advance directive); decisional with mild forgetfulness — capacity for medication decisions intact with support; daughter is healthcare proxy + lives nearby + helps with medications; goals = stay independent at home, stop falling + 'foggy' spells, simplify her medications
patient.allergies: Sulfa (rash, remote); penicillin (reports 'upset stomach' — likely intolerance, not true allergy — clarify)
patient.admitDate: Admitted via the ED after a witnessed FALL at home while getting up from a chair (no syncope reported, but lightheaded on standing); found to have ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION, a fingerstick glucose of 48 mg/dL on arrival (symptomatic HYPOGLYCEMIA), and 2 weeks of intermittent NEW CONFUSION/'fogginess' + constipation + dry mouth + daytime drowsiness per her daughter. She takes 12 medications. Review reveals POLYPHARMACY with several POTENTIALLY INAPPROPRIATE MEDICATIONS (Beers) and a likely PRESCRIBING CASCADE. Admitted for fall evaluation, glucose stabilization, and a comprehensive interprofessional medication review/reconciliation with deprescribing and safe transition-of-care planning.
patient.room: Geriatric/medical unit; fall precautions; interprofessional team (hospitalist/geriatrician + clinical pharmacist + nurse + physical/occupational therapy + social work + her PCP and community pharmacy contacted); daughter involved with a 'brown-bag' bring-in of all medications

vitals.bp: 148/84 lying → 116/64 standing with HR 96 + reproduced lightheadedness (orthostatic hypotension: SBP drop 32, symptomatic)
vitals.temp: 36.7°C; fingerstick GLUCOSE 48 mg/dL (symptomatic hypoglycemia — diaphoretic + shaky + confused), treated with oral carbohydrate → repeat 96; alert but mildly confused (daughter says this is new over ~2 weeks); dry oral mucosa; mild bruising over right hip (no fracture on imaging); gait unsteady

36.6°C; glucose stable after holding the long-acting sulfonylurea; mentation slightly clearer; 'BROWN-BAG' medication reconciliation completed with daughter — 12 medications identified including duplicates/PRNs and OTCs she hadn't reported; pharmacist + geriatrician reviewing against Beers/STOPP-START; orthostatic + anticholinergic + hypoglycemia contributors flagged

vitals.bp: 138/80 lying → 130/76 standing (orthostasis improving as offending agents reduced)
vitals.temp: 36.6°C; interventions: glyburide (long-acting sulfonylurea) stopped + switched to a safer agent with individualized, relaxed glycemic target; diphenhydramine (sleep) and oxybutynin (urge incontinence) discontinued to reduce anticholinergic burden + non-drug measures substituted; lorazepam PRN being TAPERED (not stopped abruptly); the amlodipine→ankle-edema→furosemide prescribing cascade recognized (edema was a drug effect, not heart failure) → diuretic deprescribed + antihypertensives rationalized; bowel regimen + hydration for constipation; confusion clearing; no further hypoglycemia

36.6°C; mentation back to baseline per daughter; glucose stable in individualized target; dry mouth + constipation resolving; PT/OT cleared safe gait with a reviewed assistive device + home-safety recommendations; START review added an indicated bone-protection regimen (calcium/vitamin D + osteoporosis therapy) + ensured indicated statin continued; regimen reduced from 12 to 7 medications with simplified once-daily dosing where possible

36.6°C; stable + at baseline mentation + no hypoglycemia + steady gait; reconciled single plain-language medication list prepared (what was stopped/changed and why) + teach-back with patient + daughter completed; pill organizer + simplified schedule arranged; changes communicated to PCP + community pharmacy; follow-up scheduled; ready for safe discharge home with daughter support

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the 12-medication list + adverse drug events
labs.v: 80 y/o woman R. Whitfield admitted after a witnessed FALL at home (rose from a chair, became lightheaded, fell — no clear syncope) with ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION (lying 148/84 → standing 116/64, SBP drop 32 mmHg, symptomatic) and an arrival fingerstick GLUCOSE of 48 mg/dL (symptomatic HYPOGLYCEMIA — diaphoretic, tremulous, confused; treated with oral carbohydrate → 96). Her daughter reports ~2 weeks of NEW intermittent CONFUSION/'fogginess,' daytime DROWSINESS, DRY MOUTH, and worsening CONSTIPATION. Hip imaging after the fall shows soft-tissue bruising, NO fracture. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, osteoarthritis, urge urinary incontinence, insomnia, GERD, osteoporosis (prior DEXA), mild cognitive forgetfulness, prior peripheral edema. SOCIAL: widowed, lives alone in a single-level home; daughter lives nearby and assists with medications + groceries; former librarian; values independence; mild baseline forgetfulness but manages with a written list; uses reading glasses; mild hand arthritis (some difficulty with bottle caps). HOME MEDICATIONS ('brown-bag' review with daughter revealed 12, including OTCs she had not reported to clinicians): (1) amlodipine 10 mg daily (HTN); (2) lisinopril 20 mg daily (HTN/diabetes); (3) furosemide 20 mg daily (started months ago for 'ankle swelling'); (4) glyburide 10 mg daily (long-acting sulfonylurea, T2DM); (5) metformin 1000 mg twice daily (T2DM); (6) oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily (urge incontinence — anticholinergic); (7) diphenhydramine 50 mg at bedtime (OTC, for sleep — anticholinergic); (8) lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN (anxiety/sleep — benzodiazepine, taken most nights); (9) omeprazole 20 mg daily (long-term, GERD); (10) ibuprofen 400 mg PRN (OTC, for knees — NSAID, taken often); (11) acetaminophen PRN; (12) a multivitamin. ADVERSE DRUG EVENTS evident: SYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA (glyburide — a Beers PIM, prolonged hypoglycemia in elderly), ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION + FALL (additive from amlodipine + lisinopril + furosemide volume depletion + benzodiazepine), NEW CONFUSION/DROWSINESS + DRY MOUTH + CONSTIPATION + falls (high ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN from oxybutynin + diphenhydramine, plus benzodiazepine sedation), and an evident PRESCRIBING CASCADE (amlodipine → ankle edema misread as fluid overload → furosemide added). The plan: stabilize, then a COMPREHENSIVE INTERPROFESSIONAL MEDICATION REVIEW (Beers/STOPP-START), DEPRESCRIBING, anticholinergic-burden reduction, full RECONCILIATION, and safe transition-of-care teaching.
labs.r: An older adult on 12 medications presents with the classic harms of polypharmacy: drug-induced hypoglycemia (glyburide), orthostatic hypotension + fall (additive antihypertensives/diuretic/benzodiazepine), and confusion/dry mouth/constipation (anticholinergic burden), plus a prescribing cascade (amlodipine edema → furosemide). RN role: obtain an accurate full medication history, recognize ADEs/cascades, and drive reconciliation + deprescribing.

labs.n: Beers Criteria PIMs + anticholinergic burden in this patient
labs.v: Applying the BEERS CRITERIA (AGS list of Potentially Inappropriate Medications in adults ≥65) to her regimen identifies several PIMs: (1) GLYBURIDE — a long-acting sulfonylurea that causes PROLONGED HYPOGLYCEMIA in older adults (Beers: avoid) → directly explains her glucose of 48; safer options include shorter-acting agents and individualized, RELAXED glycemic targets in frail elders (avoid tight control + sliding-scale-only insulin, which are also flagged); (2) DIPHENHYDRAMINE — a first-generation antihistamine with strong ANTICHOLINERGIC effects (Beers: avoid; especially for insomnia) → confusion, sedation, dry mouth, constipation, urinary issues, falls; (3) OXYBUTYNIN — an anticholinergic for urge incontinence (Beers: use with caution/avoid; high anticholinergic burden) → additive cognitive + constipation + dry-mouth + fall risk; (4) LORAZEPAM (benzodiazepine) — Beers: avoid in older adults (falls, fractures, cognitive impairment, delirium, sedation) and never stop abruptly (taper); (5) IBUPROFEN (NSAID) — Beers: avoid chronic use (GI bleeding, AKI, fluid retention/HF, BP elevation — and may be feeding the BP/edema problem); (6) long-term OMEPRAZOLE (PPI) — Beers: avoid >8 weeks without clear indication (C. difficile, fractures, B12/Mg deficiency). ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN: oxybutynin + diphenhydramine together (plus any others) produce a high CUMULATIVE anticholinergic load → her NEW CONFUSION, dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary issues, and fall risk are partly explained by this additive burden; reducing the total anticholinergic burden (stopping both, substituting non-anticholinergic or non-drug alternatives) is a high-value intervention that often reverses 'dementia-like' symptoms. RENAL/PK CAUTION: estimate renal function (CrCl/eGFR) — a 'normal' creatinine in a low-muscle-mass 80-year-old can mask reduced clearance → review renally cleared drugs (metformin dose by eGFR; gabapentinoids; many others) and apply 'start low, go slow.' Note that the Beers Criteria are a guide, not absolute rules — decisions are individualized to the patient's goals, indications, and risk/benefit.
labs.r: Beers PIMs in this patient: glyburide (hypoglycemia), diphenhydramine + oxybutynin (anticholinergic — confusion/falls), lorazepam (falls/cognition; taper), chronic NSAID (GI/renal/BP), long-term PPI. High cumulative ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN explains much of her new confusion/dry mouth/constipation. Estimate renal function (mask of low muscle mass) + 'start low, go slow.' RN role: recognize PIMs + anticholinergic burden + advocate for safer alternatives.

labs.v: PRESCRIBING CASCADE in this patient: she was started on AMLODIPINE (a dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker) for hypertension; amlodipine commonly causes dose-dependent peripheral/ANKLE EDEMA (a vasodilatory effect, NOT fluid overload/heart failure); her ankle swelling was misinterpreted as volume overload and treated by ADDING FUROSEMIDE (a loop diuretic) — which then contributed to ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION, volume depletion, electrolyte loss, and urinary urgency/incontinence (which may have driven the oxybutynin prescription — another cascade link). The correct fix is to treat the CAUSE: reduce/stop or substitute the amlodipine (or accept/limit the edema), which allows the furosemide to be DEPRESCRIBED, improving her orthostasis. Recognizing that a 'new symptom' may be a DRUG EFFECT (not a new disease) is the key to breaking cascades. DEPRESCRIBING PRINCIPLES: (1) compile the full accurate list + indications; (2) identify drugs that are inappropriate, harmful, duplicative, lacking a current indication, or not aligned with the patient's goals/life expectancy; (3) PRIORITIZE which to stop by risk/benefit (here: glyburide, diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, the furosemide cascade, chronic NSAID, and a benzodiazepine taper rise to the top); (4) change ONE thing at a time when feasible so effects can be attributed; (5) TAPER drugs with withdrawal/rebound risk rather than stopping abruptly — benzodiazepines (lorazepam → gradual taper to avoid withdrawal/seizures/rebound anxiety/insomnia), beta-blockers (rebound tachycardia/ischemia), clonidine (rebound hypertension), SSRIs/SNRIs (discontinuation syndrome), opioids + gabapentinoids, PPIs (rebound acid hypersecretion), corticosteroids (adrenal insufficiency); (6) MONITOR for withdrawal and for recurrence of the original condition, with a plan to re-address if needed; (7) substitute NON-DRUG measures (sleep hygiene + stimulus control for insomnia instead of diphenhydramine/benzodiazepine; pelvic-floor/bladder training + scheduled voiding for incontinence instead of oxybutynin; topical analgesics/acetaminophen + PT for osteoarthritis instead of chronic NSAID); (8) align with the patient's GOALS (she prioritizes staying independent + clearing her 'fogginess' + not falling). Also apply START (prescribing OMISSIONS): she has osteoporosis → ensure bone-protective therapy (calcium/vitamin D + a bisphosphonate or other agent) and confirm an indicated statin is continued — under-treatment is also a medication problem.
labs.r: Prescribing cascade: amlodipine → ankle edema (a drug effect, not heart failure) → furosemide (→ orthostasis); fix by treating the cause + deprescribing the diuretic. Deprescribe by priority, one change at a time, TAPER withdrawal-prone drugs (benzodiazepines/beta-blockers/PPI/SSRI/opioids), substitute non-drug measures, monitor, and align with goals; use START to add indicated omissions (bone protection, statin). RN role: spot cascades + support safe tapering + non-drug alternatives.

labs.n: Medication reconciliation at transitions of care
labs.v: MEDICATION RECONCILIATION is the formal safety process of creating the most accurate possible list of EVERYTHING the patient actually takes and comparing it against orders at EVERY transition of care (admission, transfer, discharge) — a Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal and the single most effective system safeguard against medication errors. WHAT TO CAPTURE for each drug: name, dose, route, frequency, indication, and adherence — and crucially include OTC medications (NSAIDs, antihistamines, antacids), HERBALS/SUPPLEMENTS (which interact — e.g., St. John's wort, vitamin K, fish oil), PRN medications (how often actually taken), eye drops, inhalers, patches, and topicals (patients often omit these). USE MULTIPLE SOURCES because any single source is frequently incomplete or inaccurate: the patient + FAMILY/CAREGIVER, the ACTUAL BOTTLES ('BROWN-BAG' review — have them bring everything in), the COMMUNITY PHARMACY fill records, the PCP/prior records, the discharge list from a recent hospitalization, and the state Prescription Drug Monitoring Program (controlled substances). DISCREPANCIES to resolve: OMISSIONS (a needed home drug not ordered), DUPLICATIONS (two drugs of the same class, or brand + generic), DOSING errors, drug-drug/drug-disease INTERACTIONS, and drugs without a current indication. This patient's brown-bag review uncovered OTC diphenhydramine and ibuprofen she had not reported and a PRN benzodiazepine taken nightly — information that changed the whole assessment. TRANSITIONS OF CARE are the highest-risk window for errors and readmissions; at DISCHARGE the reconciled list must be turned into a SINGLE, plain-language patient medication list that clearly states WHAT WAS STOPPED OR CHANGED AND WHY (so another clinician or pharmacy doesn't 'helpfully' restart a deprescribed drug), communicated to the PCP and community pharmacy, with timely follow-up arranged. NURSING ROLE: the nurse is often the person who obtains the most accurate history (skilled interviewing + brown-bag + caregiver), flags discrepancies to the prescriber/pharmacist, reconciles at each transition, documents clearly, and ensures the final list and rationale travel WITH the patient.
labs.r: Medication reconciliation = build the complete accurate list (Rx + OTC + herbals + PRNs + drops/inhalers/topicals, with dose/route/frequency/indication) from MULTIPLE sources (patient/caregiver, brown-bag bottles, pharmacy, PCP, PDMP) and compare against orders at EVERY transition (admission/transfer/discharge) to fix omissions/duplications/dosing/interactions. Discharge list must state what changed + why + reach PCP/pharmacy. RN role: accurate history + reconcile + flag + document.

labs.n: Transition-of-care teaching + adherence + interprofessional care; Pertinent NEGATIVES
labs.v: SAFE TRANSITION-OF-CARE TEACHING + ADHERENCE: at discharge, provide a SINGLE reconciled, plain-language medication list (large print for low vision), explicitly noting STOPPED/CHANGED drugs and WHY; use TEACH-BACK (have the patient/caregiver explain the regimen + which drugs were stopped) to confirm understanding; ADDRESS ADHERENCE BARRIERS common in elders — COST (generics, assistance programs, simplify), REGIMEN COMPLEXITY (consolidate to once-daily where possible, combination products, align dosing times), DEXTERITY (arthritis → request non-childproof caps, blister/bubble packs, easy-open containers, spacer/inhaler technique), COGNITION/MEMORY (pill organizers, reminders/alarms, caregiver involvement, blister packs), VISION (large-print labels/lists), HEALTH LITERACY + LANGUAGE (plain language, professional interpreter as needed), and the patient's GOALS/PREFERENCES (shared decision-making — some prioritize quality of life and fewer pills over long-term preventive medications). Teach the patient/caregiver to bring an updated list to every visit, to consult the pharmacist before adding OTCs/supplements, and to recognize warning signs (hypoglycemia, dizziness on standing, confusion). INTERPROFESSIONAL + PATIENT-CENTERED CARE: a PHARMACIST-led comprehensive medication review, geriatrician/PCP, nurse, PT/OT (fall risk, home safety, assistive device, dexterity aids), and social work (cost, home support), with the patient + daughter central to shared decisions. PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO hip or other fracture from the fall (imaging negative); NO acute coronary, arrhythmic, or neurologic cause of the fall identified (it is medication-related orthostasis/hypoglycemia, not a primary cardiac/CNS event — though those were screened); the ankle edema was a DRUG effect (amlodipine), NOT decompensated heart failure (no other HF signs) — important so the furosemide is correctly deprescribed rather than escalated; the 'new confusion' is largely DRUG-INDUCED (anticholinergic burden + hypoglycemia + sedatives) and improves with deprescribing — NOT assumed to be irreversible dementia; she retains decision-making capacity for medication choices with support; the penicillin 'allergy' is likely an intolerance to clarify (avoids unnecessary alternative-antibiotic harms). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: engaged, capable daughter/caregiver + brown-bag participation; reversible drug-induced problems caught early; interprofessional team with pharmacist-led review; clear patient goals (independence, fewer pills, no falls); good follow-up + communication to PCP/pharmacy; PT/OT home-safety optimization; regimen simplified 12 → 7 with safer choices + indicated additions (bone protection). EXPECTED: resolution of hypoglycemia, orthostasis, and confusion; reduced fall risk; improved adherence + quality of life; safe independent living with support.
labs.r: Transition teaching = single plain-language reconciled list noting what changed + why + teach-back + address adherence barriers (cost, complexity, dexterity, cognition, vision, literacy) + align with goals; communicate to PCP/pharmacy + follow-up. Interprofessional (pharmacist-led) + patient-centered. Negatives: no fracture, drug-induced (not primary cardiac/HF/dementia) causes — reversible. RN role: teaching + adherence support + advocacy + coordination.

nursesNote: ED — 80 y/o woman R. Whitfield admitted after a witnessed FALL at home (rose from a chair, lightheaded, fell; no clear syncope) with ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION (148/84 lying → 116/64 standing, SBP drop 32, symptomatic) and arrival GLUCOSE 48 mg/dL (symptomatic HYPOGLYCEMIA — diaphoretic/tremulous/confused; treated with oral carbohydrate → 96). Daughter reports ~2 weeks of NEW intermittent CONFUSION/'fogginess' + daytime drowsiness + dry mouth + worsening constipation. Hip imaging: bruising, NO fracture. PMH: HTN, T2DM, OA, urge incontinence, insomnia, GERD, osteoporosis, mild forgetfulness, prior ankle edema. SOCIAL: widowed, lives alone (single-level home), daughter nearby assists with meds, former librarian, values independence, mild hand arthritis (bottle-cap difficulty), uses reading glasses. BROWN-BAG medication reconciliation with daughter revealed 12 meds (incl. unreported OTCs): amlodipine 10, lisinopril 20, furosemide 20 (for 'ankle swelling'), GLYBURIDE 10 (long-acting sulfonylurea), metformin 1000 BID, OXYBUTYNIN 5 BID (anticholinergic), DIPHENHYDRAMINE 50 qhs (OTC sleep — anticholinergic), LORAZEPAM 0.5 PRN (taken most nights), omeprazole 20 (long-term), IBUPROFEN 400 PRN (OTC, frequent), acetaminophen PRN, multivitamin. ADEs identified: drug-induced HYPOGLYCEMIA (glyburide — Beers PIM), ORTHOSTASIS/FALL (additive amlodipine + lisinopril + furosemide + benzodiazepine), CONFUSION/DROWSINESS/DRY MOUTH/CONSTIPATION (high ANTICHOLINERGIC BURDEN: oxybutynin + diphenhydramine + benzodiazepine sedation), and a PRESCRIBING CASCADE (amlodipine → ankle edema misread as fluid overload → furosemide → orthostasis/urgency). PLAN: stabilize, comprehensive interprofessional medication review (Beers/STOPP-START), deprescribing, anticholinergic-burden reduction, full reconciliation, safe transition teaching. DAY 1: glucose stable after holding glyburide; mentation slightly clearer; fall precautions; pharmacist + geriatrician review; PT/OT consulted. DAY 2 deprescribing: glyburide STOPPED → safer agent + individualized relaxed glycemic target; diphenhydramine + oxybutynin DISCONTINUED (reduce anticholinergic burden) with non-drug substitutes (sleep hygiene; bladder training/scheduled voiding); lorazepam being TAPERED (not abrupt); amlodipine→edema→furosemide cascade recognized (edema is a drug effect, not HF) → furosemide DEPRESCRIBED + antihypertensives rationalized; chronic ibuprofen stopped → acetaminophen/topical + PT for OA; PPI flagged for taper; bowel regimen + hydration; orthostasis improving (138/80 → 130/76 standing); confusion clearing; no further hypoglycemia. DAY 3: mentation at baseline per daughter; glucose stable in individualized target; dry mouth/constipation resolving; PT/OT cleared safe gait + assistive device + home-safety recs; START review ADDED indicated bone protection (calcium/vitamin D + osteoporosis therapy) + confirmed indicated statin continued; regimen reduced 12 → 7 with simplified once-daily dosing where possible. DAY 4 discharge teaching: stable, baseline mentation, no hypoglycemia, steady gait; single plain-language reconciled medication list prepared NOTING what was stopped/changed and WHY; TEACH-BACK with patient + daughter; pill organizer + simplified schedule + large-print list + easy-open caps for arthritis; changes communicated to PCP + community pharmacy; follow-up scheduled; warning-sign teaching (hypoglycemia, orthostatic dizziness, confusion); discharged home with daughter support. Capacity for medication decisions intact with support; penicillin 'allergy' clarified as likely intolerance; documentation complete.
providerOrders: ADMIT to geriatric/medical unit; fall precautions
DIAGNOSIS: fall with orthostatic hypotension + drug-induced hypoglycemia + new confusion in the setting of polypharmacy with potentially inappropriate medications and a prescribing cascade
CODE STATUS: full code per advance directive; decisional with support; daughter is healthcare proxy
IMMEDIATE STABILIZATION:

Treat/monitor hypoglycemia: recheck glucose, hold the long-acting sulfonylurea, scheduled glucose monitoring, individualized (relaxed) glycemic target for a frail elder; avoid sliding-scale-only insulin
Orthostatic vital signs (lying/standing) each shift; fall precautions; PT/OT for gait + assistive device + home safety
Fall workup as indicated (ECG, orthostatics, neuro exam) — but recognize medication-related cause
MEDICATION RECONCILIATION:

Complete 'brown-bag' reconciliation with the patient + daughter; capture ALL drugs incl. OTC/herbals/supplements/PRN/eye drops/inhalers/topicals (name, dose, route, frequency, indication, actual adherence)
Use multiple sources: patient/caregiver, bottles, community pharmacy, PCP records, PDMP; resolve omissions/duplications/dosing errors/interactions
Reconcile at admission and again at discharge; clarify the penicillin 'allergy' (likely intolerance)
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICATION REVIEW (interprofessional, pharmacist-led; apply Beers + STOPP/START):

Identify potentially inappropriate medications: glyburide (hypoglycemia), diphenhydramine + oxybutynin (anticholinergic burden), lorazepam (falls/cognition), chronic NSAID (GI/renal/BP), long-term PPI
Recognize + unwind the prescribing cascade: amlodipine → ankle edema (drug effect, not HF) → furosemide → orthostasis
Estimate renal function (CrCl/eGFR — low muscle mass masks reduced clearance); renally dose-adjust; 'start low, go slow'
START review for omissions: bone-protection (calcium/vitamin D + osteoporosis therapy), confirm indicated statin; ensure no needed drug omitted
DEPRESCRIBING (planned, supervised, patient-centered):
Stop glyburide → safer glucose management with individualized target
Discontinue diphenhydramine + oxybutynin to reduce anticholinergic burden; substitute non-drug measures (sleep hygiene/stimulus control; bladder training/scheduled voiding)
TAPER lorazepam (do NOT stop abruptly — withdrawal/seizure/rebound risk)
Deprescribe furosemide after addressing the amlodipine edema cause; rationalize antihypertensives to BP goal + orthostasis
Stop chronic ibuprofen → acetaminophen/topical + PT for osteoarthritis; plan PPI taper (rebound acid)
Change one thing at a time when feasible; monitor for withdrawal + return of original condition
SYMPTOM/SUPPORTIVE CARE:
Bowel regimen + hydration for constipation; oral care for dry mouth; sleep-hygiene measures
Monitor mentation (expect improvement as anticholinergics/sedatives/hypoglycemia resolve — not assumed dementia)
INTERPROFESSIONAL TEAM:

Clinical pharmacist (comprehensive review + deprescribing plan), geriatrician/hospitalist, nurse, PT/OT (falls/home safety/dexterity aids), social work (cost/home support), PCP + community pharmacy communication
TRANSITION-OF-CARE + EDUCATION (teach-back):
Single plain-language (large-print) reconciled medication list noting WHAT WAS STOPPED/CHANGED AND WHY

Teach-back with patient + daughter; address adherence barriers (cost, complexity → simplify/once-daily, dexterity → easy-open caps/blister packs, cognition → pill organizer/reminders, vision → large print, literacy/language)
Counsel: bring updated list to every visit; consult pharmacist before any new OTC/supplement; recognize hypoglycemia/orthostasis/confusion warning signs
Communicate changes to PCP + community pharmacy; arrange timely follow-up (and consider home health)
DISCHARGE PLANNING:
Discharge home with daughter support when stable (no hypoglycemia, resolved orthostasis, baseline mentation, safe gait)
Reconciled list + rationale travels with patient; follow-up appointment; home-safety recommendations implemented

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this polypharmacy presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Polypharmacy (12 medications) with several Beers-criteria potentially inappropriate medications — a leading, often reversible cause of falls, confusion, and adverse drug events in older adults
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — polypharmacy + PIMs frame the whole presentation. RN role: recognize + drive medication review.

opts.text: Symptomatic hypoglycemia (glucose 48) from glyburide (a long-acting sulfonylurea — Beers PIM causing prolonged hypoglycemia in elders)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a drug-induced, dangerous, identifiable cause. RN role: treat/monitor glucose + flag the offending drug.

opts.text: Orthostatic hypotension + fall from additive antihypertensives + a diuretic + a benzodiazepine — reproduced on lying/standing vitals
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — additive drug-related orthostasis caused the fall. RN role: orthostatic vitals + fall precautions + identify contributors.

opts.text: High anticholinergic burden (oxybutynin + diphenhydramine, plus benzodiazepine sedation) explaining new confusion, dry mouth, constipation, and fall risk
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cumulative anticholinergic burden causes reversible 'dementia-like' symptoms. RN role: recognize + advocate to reduce burden.

opts.text: A prescribing cascade — amlodipine causing ankle edema that was misread as fluid overload and treated by adding furosemide (which worsened orthostasis)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing a cascade lets the team treat the cause + deprescribe. RN role: identify the cascade + flag it.

opts.text: Need for thorough medication reconciliation — the brown-bag review revealed unreported OTC diphenhydramine + ibuprofen and a nightly PRN benzodiazepine
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — accurate reconciliation (incl. OTC/PRN) changed the assessment. RN role: complete brown-bag reconciliation from multiple sources.

opts.text: Transition-of-care risk + adherence factors (cost, complexity, hand arthritis, vision, mild forgetfulness, caregiver involvement) for safe discharge teaching
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — transitions are the highest-risk window; adherence barriers must be addressed. RN role: reconcile + teach-back + simplify + coordinate.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa (rash) + penicillin ('upset stomach' — likely intolerance to clarify) — document and clarify
opts.rat: Allergy documentation + clarifying a likely intolerance avoids unnecessary alternative-drug harms. Relevant but not the central cue.

opts.text: The new confusion should be attributed to underlying dementia and an antipsychotic started for agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The confusion is largely DRUG-INDUCED (anticholinergic burden + hypoglycemia + sedatives) and reversible with deprescribing — not assumed dementia. Antipsychotics in older adults (especially with dementia) carry increased mortality (Beers/boxed warning) and would add harm.

opts.text: The ankle edema indicates worsening heart failure, so the furosemide dose should be increased
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The edema is a known amlodipine (CCB) effect, not heart failure (no other HF signs). Escalating furosemide perpetuates the prescribing cascade + worsens orthostasis; the fix is to address the amlodipine and deprescribe the diuretic.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What polypharmacy + geriatric pharmacology principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Older adults have altered pharmacokinetics (reduced renal/hepatic clearance, lower albumin → more free drug, altered distribution/half-life) and pharmacodynamics (increased sensitivity) → 'start low, go slow' + renal dosing; a normal-looking creatinine can mask low clearance
opts.rat: CORRECT — geriatric PK/PD framework. Foundation for safe dosing + recognizing toxicity.

opts.text: Beers Criteria flag potentially inappropriate medications (benzodiazepines, anticholinergics like diphenhydramine/oxybutynin, glyburide, chronic NSAIDs, long-term PPIs, antipsychotics in dementia) where risks usually outweigh benefits in elders
opts.rat: CORRECT — Beers PIM framework. Foundation for identifying high-risk drugs.

opts.text: A prescribing cascade occurs when a drug's adverse effect is misread as a new condition and treated with another drug (amlodipine edema → furosemide); the fix is to treat the cause, not add drugs
opts.rat: CORRECT — prescribing-cascade principle. Foundation for unwinding the regimen.

opts.text: Cumulative anticholinergic burden from multiple drugs causes additive confusion, falls, constipation, dry mouth, and urinary retention; reducing total burden often reverses symptoms
opts.rat: CORRECT — anticholinergic-burden principle. Foundation for high-value deprescribing.

opts.text: Medication reconciliation builds a complete accurate list (incl. OTC/herbals/PRN/topicals) from multiple sources and compares it against orders at every transition to fix omissions/duplications/dosing/interactions
opts.rat: CORRECT — reconciliation principle. Foundation for transition safety.

opts.text: Deprescribing is planned + patient-centered: prioritize by risk/benefit, change one thing at a time, TAPER withdrawal-prone drugs (benzodiazepines, beta-blockers, PPIs, SSRIs, opioids), substitute non-drug measures, and use START to add indicated omissions
opts.rat: CORRECT — deprescribing + STOPP/START principles. Foundation for safe optimization.

opts.text: Because all 12 medications were prescribed at some point, they should all be continued unchanged to avoid destabilizing the patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Continuing inappropriate/harmful/duplicative drugs perpetuates the ADEs (hypoglycemia, falls, confusion). Thoughtful, supervised deprescribing — not blanket continuation — is the standard.

opts.text: Abruptly stopping all flagged medications at once, including the benzodiazepine, is the safest approach
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Stopping everything at once prevents attributing effects and risks dangerous withdrawal (e.g., benzodiazepine withdrawal/seizures, rebound hypertension). Deprescribe by priority, usually one change at a time, and TAPER withdrawal-prone drugs.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Immediate safety r/t hypoglycemia + orthostatic falls — treat/monitor glucose, hold the offending sulfonylurea, orthostatic vitals, fall precautions, and stabilize
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Acute, dangerous, drug-induced instability (hypoglycemia + fall risk) comes first. RN role: stabilize + protect.

opts.text: Accurate medication reconciliation r/t the incomplete/unsafe regimen — brown-bag review from multiple sources capturing OTC/PRN/supplements to reveal the true list
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. You cannot fix what you cannot see; an accurate list underpins every decision. RN role: thorough reconciliation.

opts.text: Interprofessional medication review + deprescribing r/t Beers PIMs, anticholinergic burden, and the prescribing cascade — pharmacist-led review, prioritized stops, taper withdrawal-prone drugs
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Optimizing the regimen resolves the underlying cause of her presentation. RN role: support review + deprescribing + advocate.

opts.text: Symptom + functional support r/t constipation/dry mouth/gait — bowel regimen, hydration, oral care, PT/OT for falls + home safety + dexterity aids
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Supports recovery + reduces fall risk + comfort. RN role: symptom management + PT/OT coordination.

opts.text: Transition-of-care teaching + adherence r/t safe discharge — reconciled plain-language list (what changed + why), teach-back, address cost/complexity/dexterity/vision/cognition, communicate to PCP/pharmacy
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + essential for sustained safety. RN role: teach-back + simplify + coordinate continuity.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: Treat + monitor hypoglycemia, hold/stop glyburide, and switch to safer glucose management with an individualized (relaxed) glycemic target for a frail elder
opts.rat: Addresses the dangerous drug-induced hypoglycemia (Beers PIM). RN role: glucose management + flag the drug.

opts.text: Obtain orthostatic vitals, implement fall precautions, and identify/reduce additive contributors (antihypertensives + diuretic + benzodiazepine)
opts.rat: Targets the orthostatic fall mechanism. RN role: monitor + protect + identify contributors.

opts.text: Complete a brown-bag medication reconciliation from multiple sources (patient/caregiver, bottles, pharmacy, PCP, PDMP), including OTC/herbals/PRN
opts.rat: Reveals the true regimen + discrepancies. RN role: thorough reconciliation.

opts.text: Reduce anticholinergic burden by discontinuing diphenhydramine + oxybutynin and substituting non-drug measures (sleep hygiene; bladder training/scheduled voiding)
opts.rat: High-value deprescribing that reverses confusion/dry mouth/constipation. RN role: support + teach alternatives.

opts.text: Recognize the amlodipine→edema→furosemide cascade and deprescribe the furosemide after addressing the amlodipine, rationalizing antihypertensives to BP goal + orthostasis
opts.rat: Unwinds the cascade + improves orthostasis. RN role: flag the cascade + monitor BP/orthostasis.

opts.text: Taper the lorazepam gradually rather than stopping it abruptly, and monitor for withdrawal
opts.rat: Benzodiazepines must be tapered (withdrawal/seizure/rebound risk). RN role: supervised taper + monitoring.

opts.text: Apply START to add indicated omissions (bone protection with calcium/vitamin D + osteoporosis therapy; confirm indicated statin) and provide a reconciled discharge list with teach-back
opts.rat: Deprescribing also means correcting under-prescribing + safe transition. RN role: ensure indicated therapy + teach-back.

opts.text: Continue all 12 medications unchanged to avoid destabilizing the patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — perpetuates the hypoglycemia, falls, and confusion. Thoughtful deprescribing is the standard, not blanket continuation.

opts.text: Increase the furosemide dose to treat the ankle edema as presumed heart failure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the edema is an amlodipine effect, not HF; escalating the diuretic perpetuates the cascade + worsens orthostasis.

opts.text: Start an antipsychotic for the new confusion and add zolpidem for sleep
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — adds Beers PIMs (antipsychotics increase mortality in dementia; Z-drugs cause falls/cognitive impairment) to drug-induced, reversible confusion. Reduce offending drugs instead + use non-drug sleep measures.

opts.text: Abruptly discontinue every flagged medication at once, including the benzodiazepine, on the day of admission
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — risks dangerous withdrawal + prevents attributing effects. Deprescribe by priority, generally one change at a time, and taper withdrawal-prone drugs.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stabilize immediate safety — recheck/treat glucose + hold glyburide, obtain orthostatic vitals, institute fall precautions
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Resolve the acute drug-induced dangers. RN role: stabilize + protect.

opts.text: Complete an accurate brown-bag medication reconciliation with the daughter from multiple sources, including OTC/PRN/supplements
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Establish the true regimen before changes. RN role: reconcile.

opts.text: Collaborate on the pharmacist-led review (Beers/STOPP-START) to deprescribe PIMs, reduce anticholinergic burden, unwind the cascade, and taper withdrawal-prone drugs
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Optimize the regimen to fix the root cause. RN role: support review + deprescribing + advocate.

opts.text: Provide symptom + functional support (bowel regimen, hydration, oral care) and engage PT/OT for gait, home safety, and dexterity aids; monitor mentation improvement
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Support recovery + reduce fall risk. RN role: symptom care + PT/OT coordination.

opts.text: Reconcile + teach at discharge — single plain-language list (what changed + why), teach-back, address adherence barriers, and communicate to PCP/pharmacy with follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Last/ongoing. Secures sustained safety across the transition. RN role: teach-back + simplify + coordinate continuity.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1: hypoglycemia resolved after holding glyburide, glucose stable on monitoring, brown-bag reconciliation completed revealing the true 12-drug list, pharmacist/geriatrician review underway
opts.rat: Acute danger resolved + accurate list obtained + review begun. Foundation for optimization.

opts.text: Day 2: glyburide stopped (safer agent), diphenhydramine + oxybutynin discontinued, lorazepam tapering, furosemide cascade unwound; orthostasis improving and confusion clearing with no further hypoglycemia
opts.rat: Deprescribing is reversing the drug-induced problems. Expected positive trajectory.

opts.text: Day 3: mentation back to baseline, glucose stable in individualized target, dry mouth/constipation resolving, safe gait with PT/OT, START additions made; regimen reduced 12 → 7
opts.rat: Symptoms resolved + regimen optimized + indicated therapy added. Strong recovery.

opts.text: Day 4: stable at baseline, reconciled plain-language list with teach-back completed, adherence aids arranged, changes communicated to PCP/pharmacy → safe discharge home
opts.rat: Safe transition with confirmed understanding + continuity. Excellent outcome.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the team continued all 12 medications and added an antipsychotic for the confusion and zolpidem for sleep; the patient becomes more sedated, falls again, and remains hypoglycemic
opts.rat: ⚠️ Adding PIMs to an unaddressed harmful regimen worsens sedation, falls, and hypoglycemia. Demonstrates why deprescribing — not adding drugs — is correct.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the ankle edema was treated by increasing furosemide; the patient develops worse orthostatic hypotension, hypokalemia, and another fall
opts.rat: ⚠️ Perpetuating the prescribing cascade (treating a drug effect as HF) worsens orthostasis + electrolytes + falls. Demonstrates the need to treat the cause + deprescribe.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the lorazepam was stopped abruptly on admission; the patient develops tremor, agitation, insomnia, and rebound anxiety from benzodiazepine withdrawal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abrupt benzodiazepine discontinuation causes withdrawal. Demonstrates why withdrawal-prone drugs must be TAPERED, not stopped suddenly.

opts.text: Two weeks post-discharge (follow-up): no further falls or hypoglycemia, mentation clear, adherent to the simplified 7-drug regimen with a pill organizer, and the PCP/pharmacy have the updated list
opts.rat: Sustained benefit from optimization + transition support + continuity. Standard ideal longer-term outcome.

opts.text: On the morning of admission, before any medication changes, repeat lying/standing vitals still show symptomatic orthostasis essentially the same as on arrival
opts.rat: Before the offending drugs are reduced, the orthostasis persists unchanged; reassessment after deprescribing is needed to judge the trajectory.
